mnemonics.title: chdir Success/Failure
mnemonics.content: CHDIR returns ZERO on success (not one), NEGATIVE (-1) on failure - remember: Z ero for success, N egative for failure (Z to the left of N on keyboard)

mnemonics.title: Error Codes Made Easy
mnemonics.content: E NOENT = No Entry (file doesn't exist); E ACCESS = Access denied (permissions); E NOTDIR = Not a directory (wrong type)

mnemonics.title: Permission Needed
mnemonics.content: CHDIR needs EXECUTE (x), not READ or WRITE - Think 'eXecute to traverse through directories'

acronyms.usage: The default directory for resolving relative paths in a process
Returned when the specified path or directory component does not exist
Returned when permission is denied on some component of the path
Returned when a path component exists but is not a directory (e.g., a file)
Shell environment variable showing current directory

visualTips.visualization: Imagine a tree with root at top. chdir moves your 'focus' from one branch to another. The '.' is where you are, '..' is one level up. Relative paths navigate like directions: './subdir' goes down one level.
Draw a family tree: Parent has CWD -> spawns Child (inherits CWD) -> Child calls chdir (changes only its own CWD) -> Parent's CWD remains unchanged. Like changing your own address, not your parents'.
Absolute path: Full GPS address from root (/home/user/docs). Relative path: Directions from current location (../images/pic.jpg means go up one level, then into images).

analogies.analogy: CWD is like your current location in a building. When someone asks 'where is the bathroom?', you give directions relative to where you are. chdir is like physically walking to a different floor/room - you now give directions from that new location.
chdir changes your 'viewpoint' for all relative path operations, similar to how changing your position in a maze changes which directions seem 'forward' or 'backward'.
Open file descriptors are like bookmarks in a book. Changing directories (chdir) doesn't move or invalidate your bookmarks - they're still at the exact same pages regardless of where you're standing.
